content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Language Pair-Aware Prompts for Multilingual and Low-Resource LLM Tasks",
  "problem": "Current LLM prompting techniques underperform for low-resource languages and in code-switching scenarios due to monolingual prompt bias and lack of context adaptation.",
  "motivation": "Effective multilingual NLP requires robust LLM performance across both high- and low-resource languages, but existing prompts often fail in these settings. As LLMs are increasingly used globally, new methods are needed to dynamically adapt to linguistic variation and resource scarcity.",
  "method": "We propose a prompting method that prepends to each task prompt a lightweight, automatically generated Language Pair Descriptor (LPD) encoding typological, script, and resource-level metadata for the source and target languages. LPD tokens are generated using a compact taxonomy (e.g., 'src=amh, tgt=eng, script=Ge'ez, typology=Semitic, resource=low') and can be constructed for any language pair. During inference, these descriptors guide the LLM to activate relevant multilingual knowledge and adjust for low-resource signals. The method is model-agnostic and requires no retraining.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on the FLORES-200 and AmericasNLI datasets, comparing zero-shot and few-shot performance with and without LPD prompts for both high- and low-resource language pairs. Baselines will include vanilla (no LPD), English-only, and translation-meta-prompts. Metrics: BLEU for translation, accuracy/F1 for NLI. Statistical significance tests will determine robustness."
}
